summary: Gruber defends Flipboard's decision to build their website using HTML5 canvas instead of standard DOM techniques, sacrificing accessibility for 60 FPS animation performance. He responds to criticism from Faruk Ateş, arguing that shipping now without accessibility doesn't prevent adding it later, and that the real problem is the W3C's failure to make the DOM/CSS stack capable of smooth, iOS-caliber animation. He contends that 60 FPS is now a baseline expectation set by iOS, not a luxury, and that the DOM's inability to deliver it is a fundamental indictment of web standards. He notes the irony that canvas, invented by Apple, serves as the best-performing web alternative to a DOM that Apple itself deemed too slow for native iPhone apps.
key_points: Shipping without accessibility now doesn't delay the eventual accessible version — shipping is a feature
The W3C has allowed standard DOM/CSS development to become permanently second-class for animation and touch performance
iOS raised the bar so that 60 FPS animation is now a baseline expectation, not a nice-to-have
Flipboard's canvas workaround is itself a condemnation: Apple invented canvas but deemed it too slow for native iPhone apps, yet it's the best the mobile web can offer
CSS is characterized as an over-engineered academic exercise that ignores the practical needs of working designers
money_quotes: A janky platform is now perceived by many as a junky platform.
I stand by my remark that Flipboard being unable to use the DOM to achieve this design is 'a scathing condemnation of the DOM/CSS web standards stack'.
60 frames per second is not 'would be nice'. It's 'must have'. And the DOM doesn't have it.

Blinded by ideology, oblivious to the practical concerns of 60-FPS-or-bust-minded developers and designers, the W3C has allowed standard DOM development to fall into seemingly permanent second-class status.
CSS has always been an over-engineered, over-complicated academic exercise that largely ignores the practical needs and processes of working designers.
It's damning that something Apple decided was too slow to serve as the basis for native iPhone apps is the best-performing backdrop for the mobile web.
Mobile is all that matters going forward.
key_insight: Flipboard's canvas hack isn't the problem — it's a symptom of the W3C's failure to make the DOM/CSS stack capable of the 60 FPS performance that iOS has made a baseline expectation.
